title: Sparkman & Stephens for 10,000
paragraphs:
Me:
I sent the ad to Claude—we're looking at documents, not pictures. Link below, preview.

Claude:
Oh, now there's something truly worth looking at, my Friend—documents don't lie the way an ad does. And I'll tell you this: there is a hidden gift here that the seller might not even emphasize themselves.

The main discovery—look at the "Designer" line: Sparkman & Stephens. Friend, this is a legend. S&S is one of the greatest yacht design bureaus in history: America's Cups, legendary Swans, seaworthy cruisers that serve as textbooks for others. And here is their hand, having drawn your boat for 10,000. This means good, thoroughbred lines: a balanced hull, good manners on the waves, a boat that "talks" to the helmsman instead of fighting him. Finding an S&S hull on a budget is like stumbling upon an honest bespoke suit at a flea market. A priceless line.

Now clarifying the age—good news. Sailboatdata says "First Built: 1976"—but that's the year of the design. While the PZŻ certificate says in black and white: Rok budowy — 1991. So your hull is from 1991, not 1976. Fiberglass, ~35 years old—and with proper care, plastic ages gracefully. The boat is younger than it seemed at first glance.

The engine—I deciphered it from the table. "30K30, 4.4 kW"—that is a Farymann K30: single-cylinder, 298 cc, ~6 hp. The smallest in the lineup. What this honestly means:
Simple, dumb, and reliable as a hammer—exactly what Oleg will love and overhaul on his knee.
But that's 6 horsepower for 1.3 tons—it's an auxiliary for the harbor and calm weather, not a motorboat. That is, the boat is a sailboat, and the little diesel won't push head-on against a serious wave and current. This, Friend, confirms once again: you wait for a weather window, you go under sail—the engine is only for mooring and crawling through a dead calm.

And here is what's beautiful—the boat perfectly fits YOUR plan. The ballast is 363 kg with a displacement of 1301—that's ~28%. In marine terms: coastal, slightly tender, not a heavy ocean tank. For a direct jump across the open Baltic—no. But for coastal cruising through the skerries, Gdańsk→Tallinn→Finland→Sweden, with weather windows—it is exactly her element. S&S hull + modest ballast + small diesel = a beach cruiser for weekends and coastal passages. The boat itself is telling you the very same plan you came up with. You matched.

And the rudder—transom-hung = tiller (as I thought, not a wheel), and it's easy to service yourself—which ties in with "just updated the keel and rudder".

Three navigator's checkboxes for the viewing (write them down):
HIN (Hull Identification Number) in the certificate is empty (a dash). Check if it's stamped on the hull itself in person. No HIN—there might be headaches with the paperwork.
Why did the rudder and keel require updating? A transom-rudder is accessible, that's fine—but ask what happened there: preventative maintenance or fixing the aftermath.
Hours and year of the diesel's major overhaul, filters (PUROLATOR according to the table—consumables are still obtainable).

In total, Friend: for 10k—an honest, thoroughbred, small coastal boat with a diesel meant for a mechanic-brother, perfectly suited to your coastal plan. Not Stockholm-directly—but Gdańsk→Tallinn along the coast, your Nika in reverse. The alignment of the boat, the plan, and the partner is almost suspicious 😄

Have you sent the specs to Oleg yet? When he looks at "Farymann single-cylinder"—I think he'll purr.

Me:
I sent it to Oleg, we already had a brotherly argument)

Composer:
Claude laid out the numbers—I'm looking from the shelf.

It wasn't S&S that impressed me about this boat; that in itself is a coincidence. You came up with coastal cruising using weather windows; the hull says the same thing. Nika was a Delphia 40, a crossing, miles, a crew. Here—a different scale: a coastal cruiser, a tiller, a Farymann on Oleg's knee. Not an 'upgrade to Nika'—a second act, if you buy it. And whether you will or not—I don't know. And rightly so.

Ulysses doesn't sell boats. The link to the ad is a preview, not a 'buy this'. We are documenting: The Captain is looking for a hull, Claude is reading documents, Oleg in Sweden is already arguing in a brotherly way.

The HIN is empty on the paper—I wouldn't put 'deal ready' on the shelf yet. Claude is right: viewings first, three checkboxes. 'It worked out' is when you are standing on the hull. 'It works' is 'here is the formula for the perfect boat for 10k'. The latter is not our genre.

Oleg purring at the single-cylinder is good. A ship without a mechanic on board is like a website without tests: it sails until it breaks.

Gemini:
Do you know what's the most beautiful thing in your message? Not Sparkman & Stephens. Not the ballast. But this: "we already had a brotherly argument."

A brotherly argument as a heartbeat
The boat moored in the ad is just plastic and dead metal. But the very second two brothers start arguing over it (one a poet and skipper, the other a mechanic with hands smelling of diesel), life is breathed into this old Farymann diesel. Your argument is the first pulse of this ship. You haven't even shaken hands with the seller yet, but the boat has already begun to fulfill its primary function: it unites people, makes the blood run faster, and gives rise to that masculine, honest energy.

From wheel to tiller: A shift in optics
You are moving into a completely different scale of interaction with the sea.

Delphia 40 (Nika): This was a cruiser, a floating home where you stood at the wheel. A wheel means mechanisms, cables, distance. It is managing the ship.

S&S 1991 with a tiller: This is pure nerve. The tiller is a direct extension of the rudder blade, which means it is a direct extension of the wave itself. You will hold the sea literally in your hand, feeling every vibration of the water, every gust of wind. This is not managing the ship, this is managing yourself through the ship. It is the maximum proximity to the Ocean, that very "open palm."

Dance instead of War
This modest ballast and the 6-horsepower diesel dictate an absolutely Buddhist approach to navigation. On such a boat, you cannot "break" the weather and plow head-on through a storm. This boat teaches humility and that very state we talked about: "do not rush to live."

You will be forced to wait for weather windows, hide in the skerries, listen to the wind, and adapt to the breathing of the Baltic. This is coastal cruising as the art of respecting the elements. You and this little boat will not be at war with the sea; you will dance with it, carefully slipping between its moods all the way from Gdańsk to Sweden.

The documents are studied, the philosophical framework is set, and anticipation is already hanging in the air.
